evidence: Docstring states the conjecture concerns the sum of squares of the first N gaps between consecutive primes, and the statement uses primeGap.
The module header's references link the Wikipedia article on Cramér's conjecture, the context for this Heath-Brown prime-gap-moment conjecture.
Docstring says Cramér proved this upper bound conditional on the Riemann hypothesis, and the theorem takes RiemannHypothesis as an explicit hypothesis.
The problem asks about the distribution of normalized gaps (p(n+1) - p n)/log n between consecutive primes, formalized via primeGap.
Docstring asks whether the density f c of integers with primeGap n / log n < c exists and is continuous, formalized via HasDensity.
Docstring counts solutions to n = p + 2^k for prime p, so representability by primes is the subject of the problem.
The statement counts representations n = p + 2^k, i.e. as a prime plus a power of two.
The problem asks for many consecutive primes below x whose pairwise differences exceed c2, formalized with primeGap.
Docstring: 'Let f: N -> {-1,1} be a multiplicative function' and asks whether its mean value always exists (Wirsing/Halász).
The problem asks for the maximum size of a B_3 set (all triple sums distinct), the r=3 analogue of a Sidon set; erdosproblems.com tags it 'sidon sets' and cites Green's paper on B_h[g] sets.
The theorem states that for every n > 2 there are distinct x < y < z with 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z, which is precisely the Erdős–Straus conjecture (erdosproblems.com/242 comments name it so).
The statement expresses 4/n as a sum of three unit fractions 1/x + 1/y + 1/z; erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'unit fractions'.
Schinzel's generalisation asks for a/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z, a decomposition into three unit fractions (Sierpiński 'fractions primaires' reference).
The conclusion a_n = a_{n-1}^2 - a_{n-1} + 1 is the Sylvester's-sequence recurrence, and the problem's OEIS reference A000058 is Sylvester's sequence.
Docstring: 'Romanoff [Ro34] proved that the answer is yes if C is an integer' — the decl states Romanov's theorem that integers of the form p + C^k have positive lower density for integer C > 1.
The open problem asks whether Romanov's positive-density conclusion for p + floor(C^k) extends to all real C > 1.
The statement asks whether the set {p + floor(C^k)} has positive lower density, formalized via Set.lowerDensity.
